images.caption: Figure 1:One Click per Cell Type is All You Need.Pretrained models fail to identify unseen types and their performance is limited to a specific cell type (red dashed boxes). While SAM3[2]generalizes, it requires per-instance clicks (e.g., 245). Our CoP achieves 92.7% of the upper bound performance[2]with only 3 clicks.

Figure 2:From 245 Clicks to 3: Group Prompting.Manual prompting requires one click per instance; our group prompting propagates each click to all same-type instances, reaching92.7%of the upper bound with81.7×\mathbf{81.7\times}fewer prompts.

Figure 3:Overview of Chain-of-Prompts (CoP).A frozen SAM encoder extractsFhF_{h}andFlF_{l}once per image.
For each user clickpxp_{x}(✩), HSG (Sec.˜2.1) produces initial reliable pointsℛ(0)\mathcal{R}^{(0)}via hierarchical similarity and connected-component labeling (CCL).
FPR (Sec.˜2.2) then expandsℛ(0)\mathcal{R}^{(0)}by iteratively prompting the farthest uncovered point (◆) until no new points are found. All propagated points per cell type are finally decoded into instance masks.

Figure 4:Qualitative comparison on CoNIC[6].
Fully-supervised methods miss cell populations absent from their training set (red dashed boxes), whereas CoP discovers them from a single click per type.

Figure 5:UMAP[17]of SAM’s frozen image encoder features at GT instance centroids. The UMAP embeddings are extracted from the input image used inFig.˜2. (a)FhF_{h}mixes cell types; (b)FlF_{l}groups same-type cells without any training.
